posts.name: Todd French
posts.cooked:
This wine starts out dark and rich, like my buddy @Robert.A.Jr - earthy and rustic profile, some blue fruits barely peek through, focusing more on earth and structure. In minutes a more powerfully elegant profile emerges - violets and red and black cherries, the palate has a beautiful weighty softness but with bright edges from the acidity and dusty tannic structure. Finish is long, circles back to the darker fruit and earth profile.

I really love the mouthfeel - it’s like rose petals, soft but with strength and resilience. Fruit is bright and lively on this wine, and I love how the dusty character clings just a bit to the lower gumline. Insane value, to me - I’m fully a convert to this Chateau and will definitely be on the lookout to backfill this vintage, in particular.

IMG_0404.HEIC3024×4032 2.57 MB

posts.name: Josh Grossman
posts.cooked: You just couldn’t wait 15 years… Don’t you have enough Napa cabs to fill the wait? J/K /s Thanks to @Robert.A.Jr I’m well stocked in the '15 and on of this wine and have backfilled considerably. I recently opened a 2001 which was the WOTN.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Lol. Dark and rich! That’s racist and complementary at the same time. I’m so rich I load up on QPRs like this! I think 2014 Sociando is fabulous. Bought at least two cases but letting it slumber at this point. Along the same lines, just a notch below, is 2014 Lanessan. These are wines to buy in large quantities and then thank the winemaker for making such fine wine for such values, as you enjoy them for the next 20+ years.

Really psyched you guys dig these. Such a wheelhouse wine for me. Like that pair of washed out Seven for all Mankind jeans that fit my ass like cashmere gloves on Audrey Hepburn. PS. I really have no clue what that means.

I opened a 14 Lafon Rochet on impulse the other night. It drank pretty well — I am optimistic about my various 14s.

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked: Sorry to interrupt the circle jerk, but is '14 really ready to drink? I’m concerned about the '82 Sociando Mallet I might open this holiday season.

posts.name: Todd French
posts.cooked:
Andrew_K:

Sorry to interrupt the circle jerk, but is '14 really ready to drink? I’m concerned about the '82 Sociando Mallet I might open this holiday season.

According to me, absolutely - I’m really enjoying it. Yes, the fruit is still somewhat primary, and there’s much less of the saddle leather and graphite that will inevitably emerge, but it’s so wonderful right now - complex, enjoyable, and I’ve already written down that Sociando-Mallet will be one of very few Bordeaux purchases moving forward - would rather work with wines I know for sure I’ll enjoy. I have more of this wine, not sure it will get to a full secondary phase as it’s wonderful now. Big fan.

I have more of this wine, not sure it will get to a full secondary phase as it’s wonderful now. Big fan.

That’s the thing I locked onto in your note, Todd. I have really enjoyed the 14s I’ve had for their relatively early openness. It looks like the Soc combines the best of the vintage characteristics with the style and profile usual for this house. I’ll see if I can hunt some of the 14 down. Thanks for posting

maluhia,

A big tenderloin. I’m guessing I will be cooking it since the last two times I made the brother-in-law do it, me being philosophically opposed to manual labor, he undercooked the racks of lamb and then the time before he overcooked the ribeye. I’m feeling a little pressure, I may be texting you for expertise.

posts.name: Todd French
posts.cooked:
Robert.A.Jr:

It’s the Jean Gautreau Cuvee bottling.

oh snap!!! When should I come over?

Robert.A.Jr:

A big tenderloin. I’m guessing I will be cooking it since the last two times I made the brother-in-law do it, me being philosophically opposed to manual labor, he undercooked the racks of lamb and then the time before he overcooked the ribeye. I’m feeling a little pressure, I may be texting you for expertise.

I thought there was no such thing as undercooked meat for you - the bluer, the better!

As far as expertise, you have many thousands of true experts right here, buddy - definitely search for it, but I’d definitely sous vide and sear that sucker, assures perfection all the way through, and clearly you aspire to perfection in all things but your court wardrobe. Sous vide equipment is cheap, insanely reliable, and portable. I have this one and it works brilliantly: Anova Precision® Cooker Nano 3.0 – Anova Culinary

Put a black garlic rub on it, a la @Christine_Huang, and that with some Jean Gautreau omfg soooooo good
